summary: A full Hebrew Haggadah marked with cantillation that is exceptionally useful for chant-aware traditional supplements, but not as an English beginner spine; selected sequences preserve complete ritual units and must be paired with plain-language meaning and action cues.

sourceAssessment.voice: Liturgically precise, Hebrew-forward, chant-oriented, traditional, and visually dependent on vocalization and trope marks.
bestUses: Optional Hebrew chant panels for ritually experienced hosts
Facilitator editions that explicitly teach how a passage is sung
beginnerRisks: The extracted text is principally Hebrew and cannot orient an English-only first-time host by itself.
Cantillation marks need correct font rendering and may be lost or made confusing by careless line wrapping.
Long traditional passages include assumptions and ritual details that require an accessible English explanation.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: Traditional biblical, land, temple, and Jerusalem language appears as liturgy rather than modern political advocacy. Any Jerusalem refrain must obey the project rule limiting it to a traditional selection without a social-justice override.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer, identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 source, indicate changes, and preserve ShareAlike terms. Review embedded textual material and verify that cantillation survives typesetting.

recommendedSequences.title: Chanted questions and teaching
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain the ritual actions in English first, then identify the Hebrew portions selected for chanting: the invitation, the Four Questions, the duty to tell, and the Four Children.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These consecutive source records preserve the complete opening movement of Maggid instead of extracting a trope-marked line without its ritual and narrative setting.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 tropified edition; indicate any cuts.

recommendedSequences.title: Chanted ancestry, oppression, and cry
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Give guests the Exodus arc in clear English before this selection: the family migrated, grew, was enslaved, cried out, and was heard.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The three adjacent Hebrew records form a sustained traditional exposition from ancestral migration through oppression and the response to the enslaved people’s cry.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer; retain CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution and ShareAlike requirements.

recommendedSequences.title: Plagues leading into Dayenu
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Frame the plagues with sorrow for suffering, tell guests how to diminish the cup, and explain that Dayenu shifts from catastrophe toward gratitude.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The source’s consecutive progression from redemption imagery through the plague list and rabbinic expansion into Dayenu preserves the traditional narrative turn.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and preserve the CC BY-SA 4.0 license and change notice.

recommendedSequences.title: Matzah, maror, and lived memory
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Point to the matzah and maror, summarize their meanings in English, and explain that the text asks every generation to experience the freedom story personally before moving into praise.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These adjacent passages complete the central ritual-symbol explanations, the every-generation teaching, and the source’s direct transition into Hallel.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and identify the tropified CC BY-SA 4.0 source in final credits.

recommendedSequences.title: Eating rituals into grace after meals
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Use the project’s English host directions to say exactly what each person washes, eats, and drinks; present this Hebrew only as an optional liturgical layer.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The source records follow one uninterrupted table sequence from the second cup through washing and symbolic eating into the opening of grace after the meal.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer; retain CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution, change notice, and ShareAlike terms.

recommendedSequences.title: Closing cumulative songs
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are playful cumulative songs traditionally sung at the end; provide a refrain cue and make participation optional.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Four final source-order segments preserve the complete rising pattern of Echad Mi Yodea and the continuation of Chad Gadya as a coherent closing activity.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and preserve the source’s CC BY-SA 4.0 conditions.

reasons: Title, edition, and production material belongs in credits rather than the ritual reading stream.
This eleven-word extraction fragment begins and ends mid-psalm and is not independently coherent.

notes: This source is preserved as an opt-in chant resource rather than excluded for requiring Hebrew literacy.
Every selected sequence needs a visually verified Hebrew font and a separate plain-language English explanation; the source text itself must remain exact.
All sequences remain local-agent-reviewed candidates, not human-approved runtime material.
